# Eventide H90 — MIDI control research

Research backing `internal/device/device-types/h90.yaml`. Firmware reference:
**1.7.1** (manual at `cdn.eventideaudio.com/manuals/h90/1.7.1`).

## TL;DR

- **Programs** are recalled with **standard MIDI Program Change**. Default PC
  range is **1–100**; enable **PC Offset** in the System Menu to use **0–99**.
- There is **no MIDI Bank Select (CC0/CC32)**. "Bank Up/Bank Down" are
  CC-mappable *navigation* functions (they move ±3 Programs), not Bank Select
  messages. You also **cannot change the Playlist over MIDI**.
- The H90 has **zero default MIDI CC assignments**. Every CC function must be
  user-assigned on the device (**System Menu → MIDI → Global Control**) or in
  H90 Control. The CC numbers in the YAML are therefore a **server-side
  convention**, not Eventide factory values — the H90 must be configured to
  match them.
- **Switch semantics:** Active/Bypass + trigger functions act on the CC value:
  **`>= 64` = active/engage**, **`< 64` = bypass/off**.
- **No documented public SysEx parameter API.** Deep per-algorithm parameter
  control is done via the Quick Knob / HotKnob / Performance global mappings
  (which are CC) or the H90 Control app. No SysEx controls were invented.

## Sources

| # | URL |
|---|-----|
| S1 | https://cdn.eventideaudio.com/manuals/h90/1.7.1/content/system-menu/midi.html |
| S2 | https://cdn.eventideaudio.com/manuals/h90/1.7.1/content/appendix/global.html |
| S3 | https://www.eventideaudio.com/forums/topic/h90-banks-midi-and-tuner/ |
| S4 | https://www.eventideaudio.com/forums/topic/h90-midi-programming-questions/ |
| C1 | Community convention (CC# block), eliteai "mmd-device-h90" skill: https://eliteai.tools/agent-skills/mmd-device-h90-1 |

> **Citation note on CC numbers.** Eventide publishes the list of mappable
> *functions* (S2) and the rule that there are *no defaults* (S2), but it does
> **not** publish CC numbers for them — by design they are user-chosen. The CC
> numbers below come from the community convention (C1) where available, and are
> extended contiguously for the remaining continuous params. They are clearly
> marked `# convention (no factory default)` in the YAML. **None of them are
> Eventide-defined defaults.**

## MIDI channel behaviour (S1)

- **Channel:** user-selectable 1–16; the H90 receives *and* transmits on this
  channel.
- **Receive Omni:** when on, the H90 receives on all channels simultaneously
  (overrides the receive channel); it still transmits on the configured channel.
- **Output Mode:** `Off` / `Transmit` (sends PC, CC, MIDI Clock out the DIN
  out; no thru) / `Thru` (passes DIN-in to DIN-out; H90 sends nothing).
- The channel is **not** stored in the device definition — per the project
  design it is supplied by the *binding* `(endpoint, channel, definition)`.

## Program recall (S1, S3, S4)

- Send **Program Change** to load a Program from the **currently selected
  Playlist**. PC 1 → Program 1, PC 3 → Program 3, etc.
- A Program is a pair of algorithm **presets**; you **cannot** select an
  individual preset over MIDI — build Programs ahead of time in the order you
  need (S4).
- **PC Offset** (System Menu): default off → PC **1–100**; on → PC **0–99**
  (use when other gear counts programs from 0) (S1, S4).
- **Avoid sending PC and CC simultaneously** — one may be ignored; hence the
  `settle_ms` delay between program change and CC overrides on recall (S4).

## No factory CC map — Global Control (S1, S2)

> *"The H90 has no default MIDI CC or external controller mappings, all mappings
> are customizable by the user."* (S2)

Configure on the device: **System Menu → MIDI → Global Control** — pick a
*Parameter* (Quick Knob 1) and a *CC #* (Quick Knob 2) (S1). The full list of
mappable parameters is the **Global Mapping Chart, Appendix C** (S2).

### Active/Bypass + trigger value rule (S2)

- `P Act/Byp` (toggle): a CC value **≥ 64** toggles active/bypass state.
- `P Act/Byp (M)` (momentary): CC **< 64** → bypass, CC **≥ 64** → active.
  The YAML uses the **(M)** semantics for deterministic state management
  (`bypass: 0`, `active: 127`).

## Control map

`PC` = Program Change. `CC*` = convention CC (assign on the H90; **not** a
factory default). Function names match the Global Mapping Chart (S2).

| YAML control | H90 function (S2) | Type | Addr | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `program` | Load Program | program_change | PC 1–100 (0–99 w/ offset) | range 1–100 | S1/S4 |
| `program_load` | Load | cc | CC* 20 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `program_increment` | Increment | cc | CC* 21 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `program_decrement` | Decrement | cc | CC* 22 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `program_inc_load` | Inc + Load | cc | CC* 23 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `program_dec_load` | Dec + Load | cc | CC* 24 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `bank_up` | Bank Up (±3 prog) | cc | CC* 25 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `bank_down` | Bank Down (±3 prog) | cc | CC* 26 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `program_active_bypass` | P Act/Byp (M) | cc | CC* 102 | bypass<64 / active≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `preset_a_active_bypass` | A Act/Byp (M) | cc | CC* 104 | bypass<64 / active≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `preset_b_active_bypass` | B Act/Byp (M) | cc | CC* 106 | bypass<64 / active≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `insert_1_active_bypass` | Ins 1 Act/Byp | cc | CC* 108 | bypass<64 / active≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `insert_2_active_bypass` | Ins 2 Act/Byp | cc | CC* 109 | bypass<64 / active≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `program_active_bypass_toggle` | P Act/Byp (toggle) | cc | CC* 103 | trigger ≥64 (flips state) | S2 fn |
| `preset_a_active_bypass_toggle` | A Act/Byp (toggle) | cc | CC* 105 | trigger ≥64 (flips state) | S2 fn |
| `preset_b_active_bypass_toggle` | B Act/Byp (toggle) | cc | CC* 107 | trigger ≥64 (flips state) | S2 fn |
| `hotswitch_1` | HS1 | cc | CC* 110 | off<64 / on≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `hotswitch_2` | HS2 | cc | CC* 111 | off<64 / on≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `hotswitch_3` | HS3 | cc | CC* 112 | off<64 / on≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `hotswitch_1_momentary` | HS1 (M) | cc | CC* 113 | on while ≥64 / off <64 | S2 fn |
| `hotswitch_2_momentary` | HS2 (M) | cc | CC* 114 | on while ≥64 / off <64 | S2 fn |
| `hotswitch_3_momentary` | HS3 (M) | cc | CC* 115 | on while ≥64 / off <64 | S2 fn |
| `perform_1`…`perform_6` | PERFORM 1–6 | cc | CC* 40–45 | off<64 / on≥64 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `quick_knob_1`…`quick_knob_6` | Quick Knob 1–6 | cc | CC* 50–55 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `hotknob_p` | P HotKnob | cc | CC* 60 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `hotknob_a` | A HotKnob | cc | CC* 61 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `hotknob_b` | B HotKnob | cc | CC* 62 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `program_mix` | P Mix | cc | CC* 70 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `program_in_gain` | P In Gain | cc | CC* 71 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `program_out_gain` | P Out Gain | cc | CC* 72 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `preset_a_in_gain` | A In Gain | cc | CC* 73 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `preset_a_out_gain` | A Out Gain | cc | CC* 74 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `preset_b_in_gain` | B In Gain | cc | CC* 75 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `preset_b_out_gain` | B Out Gain | cc | CC* 76 | range 0–127 | S2 fn; CC# invented (convention) |
| `tuner` | Tuner | cc | CC* 27 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1; S3 |
| `tap_tempo` | Tap Tempo | cc | CC* 80 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `mode_toggle` | Mode Toggle | cc | CC* 30 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `mode_select` | SELECT Mode | cc | CC* 31 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `mode_perform` | PERFORM Mode | cc | CC* 32 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |
| `mode_bank` | BANK Mode | cc | CC* 33 | trigger ≥64 | S2 fn / C1 |

> The per-Preset `A In/Out Gain` and `B In/Out Gain` functions (previously
> omitted) are now modelled as `preset_a_in_gain` / `preset_a_out_gain` /
> `preset_b_in_gain` / `preset_b_out_gain` (CC* 73–76), alongside the latching
> Act/Byp toggle variants (CC* 103/105/107) and the momentary HotSwitches
> (CC* 113–115). All use convention CCs. Any further Global Mapping Chart
> functions can be added the same way; deep per-algorithm parameter editing
> remains out of reach over MIDI (no documented public SysEx parameter API — see
> the H90 Control / TRPC section above).

## USB readback — system SysEx + mass storage (state verification)

For the USB readback research (verifying what a BLE-MIDI write actually landed —
see `docs/research/usb.md`), the H90 was the one device with **two plausible
readback routes**: a documented Eventide system SysEx, and the mass-storage LUN
exposed on its USB-C port. Both were investigated read-only; **both are dead ends
for non-destructive state verification.** The honest conclusion is: the H90 has
**no usable USB readback channel** — verification stays on the BLE-MIDI echo path
(or, out of scope, the undocumented H90 Control app protocol).

### Route 1 — Eventide system SysEx (documented, but NOT honored by the H90)

Eventide publishes a system SysEx for the **Factor / Space / H9** family (the
"TJ" protocol). The H90 is a newer platform, but the family protocol is the only
**documented** Eventide system "get", so it is transcribed here and was probed
against the H90 directly.

**Source:** "MIDI Sysex Messages for the Eventide Factor series pedals", V1.1
(njr, 2018) — covers TimeFactor/ModFactor/PitchFactor/Space/H9. (S5)

| # | URL |
|---|-----|
| S5 | https://www.scribd.com/document/754982112/Eventide-H9-Multi-FX-MIDI-Sysex-Messages |
| S6 | https://eventideaudio.com/downloads/midi-sysex-messages (DSP4000/Orville/7000 family — same `1C 70` framing lineage) |

**Two message families (documented):**

1. **Universal Identity Request** (standard, non-real-time):
   `F0 7E <dev> 06 01 F7` → Identity Reply
   `F0 7E 06 02 1C ff ff dd dd ss ss ss ss F7` where mfg `0x1C` = Eventide,
   `ff ff` = device family code (14-bit, LSB first), `dd dd` = family member,
   `ss ss ss ss` = software revision. `<dev>` may be `0x7F` (broadcast) or the
   unit's configured SysEx ID.
2. **Eventide proprietary**, framed:
   `F0 1C 70 <dev> <cmd> <data…> F7`
   `1C` = Eventide mfg, `70` = model id, `<dev>` = SysEx ID (`0x00` = all units),
   `<cmd>` = message code. Multi-byte values are **nibble-split** (MS-nibble
   first) because MIDI data is 7-bit; sysvar keys/values are ASCII hex/decimal.

   The **read-only "WANT" reads** (each provokes a matching DUMP) — these are the
   "system SysEx get" the readback research targets:

   | Cmd | Name | Reply | Reads |
   |-----|------|-------|-------|
   | `0x4C` | `SYSEXC_TJ_SYSVARS_WANT` | `0x4D` SYSVARS_DUMP | all system parameters (backup blob) |
   | `0x4E` | `SYSEXC_TJ_PROGRAM_WANT` | `0x4F` PROGRAM_DUMP | the current preset/program |
   | `0x48` | `SYSEXC_TJ_PRESETS_WANT` | `0x49` PRESETS_DUMP | all stored presets |
   | `0x50` | `SYSEXC_TJ_ALL_WANT` | `0x51` ALL_DUMP | SYSVARS + PRESETS combined |
   | `0x3B` | `SYSEXC_VALUE_WANT` | `0x2E` VALUE_DUMP | one sysvar by ASCII-hex key |
   | `0x31` | `SYSEXC_OBJECTINFO_WANT` | `0x2E` VALUE_DUMP | one sysvar by key (alt) |

   Read-only sysvar keys of interest (data = ASCII-hex key, e.g. `"0000"`):
   `tj_version_key` (`+0`, version string `"OSV E.M …"`), `tj_switch_key` (`+1`,
   live footswitch/jack-sense bitfield), `tj_pedal_key` (`+2`, expression pedal
   0–100). Read/write keys (bypass, MIDI channel, tempo, routing, …) live under
   the boolean (`+0x100`), byte (`+0x200`), word (`+0x300`) key bases — **writes
   are deliberately not issued**; only the WANT/VALUE_WANT reads are.

**Live result on the H90 (probed read-only, `cmd/usb-probe --device h90`):**
the H90 answers **none** of these over USB-MIDI — not the Identity Request, not
any TJ WANT. Its USB-MIDI interface carries only standard **PC / CC / MIDI Clock**
(matching the manual's MIDI section). So the documented Factor-family SysEx, while
useful as Eventide-protocol background, is **not implemented on the H90** and is
not a readback channel here. This is consistent with the TL;DR's "no documented
public SysEx parameter API" — now confirmed empirically for the system SysEx too.

### Route 2 — USB mass storage is a Recovery-Mode firmware volume, not presets

The H90 enumerates a **USB Mass Storage** interface (`/dev/sdX`, Bulk-Only; see
the descriptor in `docs/research/usb.md`). It is tempting to read presets off it,
but that interface is **not a user preset filesystem**:

- In **normal operation the LUN has no medium** — `/dev/sda` reports **0 sectors**
  (`/sys/block/sda/size` = 0); there is nothing to mount.
- The drive only mounts when the pedal is booted into **Recovery Mode** (hold
  **Select + Perform + Quick Knob 1** while inserting power; screen shows
  "Recovery Mode"). It then appears as a volume **labeled "H90 Pedal"** whose
  purpose is **firmware install**: you delete its contents and drag in the
  firmware images **`.os` + `.pak` + `.bam`**, then eject and restart. (S7)
- That makes it a **firmware-update scratch volume**, not a preset store, and it
  is **write-destructive** (the documented procedure *erases* it). For this
  research it is treated as off-limits except read-only inspection in Recovery
  Mode, and it is **not** a viable verification channel.

| # | URL |
|---|-----|
| S7 | https://cdn.eventideaudio.com/manuals/h90/1.7.1/content/appendix/startup.html (Recovery Mode / Clear State / Factory Reset) |

> Entering Recovery Mode takes the pedal **offline** (no audio, no MIDI) and is a
> power-on key combo — it cannot be triggered over USB and is unsuitable for a
> live "read current state" check.

### Where the preset "file format" actually lives (host-side, H90 Control)

The H90's Programs/Presets/Lists are managed by the **H90 Control** app
(Mac/Win/iPad) over USB or Bluetooth, and **exported as files on the host
computer** — not exposed as a device volume. The on-disk formats are:

- **`*.pgm90`** — a single **Program** (a pair of presets + routing).
- **`*.lst90`** — a **Program List / Playlist** (up to 99 Programs).
- **Device backup** — a full backup of all Lists/Programs/Presets + system
  settings (H90 Control "Backup Device"; firmware ≥ 1.7.4). (S8)

These are H90-Control container files; their internal byte layout is **not
documented by Eventide** and would need its own capture/reverse-engineering pass
(out of scope here). The point for the readback research: the "preset file
format" is a **host-side export**, reachable only through the app's protocol, so
it does not give the daemon a cheap on-device verification read.

| # | URL |
|---|-----|
| S8 | https://cdn.eventideaudio.com/manuals/h90/1.8.5/content/h90-control/edit.html (Export Program/List, Backup/Restore Device) |

### Conclusion for verification

The H90 is the **blocked** device for USB readback: USB-MIDI SysEx is not
implemented, the mass-storage LUN is a write-destructive firmware volume, and
preset files only exist host-side inside H90 Control. Until/unless the H90
Control app protocol (USB/BLE) is reverse-engineered, `verify_control` for the
H90 must rely on the **BLE-MIDI echo** model (`internal/engine/feedback.go`) like
the SL-2's blocked state. Reproduce the negative SysEx result with
`go run ./cmd/usb-probe --device h90` (read-only).

## H90 Control protocol (static analysis)

The conclusion above ("until/unless the H90 Control app protocol is reverse
engineered") prompted a **static-analysis pass on the H90 Control desktop app**
itself. This section captures what that protocol *is*, recovered without any
hardware traffic — purely from the shipped binary. It is **interoperability
research on the user's own licensed software and own device**; no traffic was
captured, nothing was patched, and the daemon's controllable surface is
unchanged (still Program Change + CC; see the TL;DR). The structured catalog
lives in `docs/research/h90-control.yaml`.

### Method & artifact

H90 Control ships as a BitRock/InstallBuilder installer (Tcl + cookfs/LZMA);
plain zlib carving only yields the installer runtime. An **unattended Wine
install** unpacks the real app, **`H90 Control.exe` 1.9.13** — an *unstripped*
JUCE/C++ x86-64 PE with full RTTI and leaked Jenkins build paths. Facts below
come from RTTI/mangled symbols, log/exception strings, the build-path module
map, and targeted disassembly (`objdump` + a capstone string-xref script;
read-only, the app was never run against the pedal).

| # | URL |
|---|-----|
| S9 | RTTI/symbols + log strings in `H90 Control.exe` 1.9.13 (static analysis; no public URL) |

### Architecture: TRPC over MIDI, FlatBuffers payloads

The app talks to the pedal with an in-house RPC it calls **TRPC** ("Tide RPC"):

- **`TRPCMessageBroker<…>`** is instantiated twice —
  `trpc::dot9::{Dot9MessageT, Dot9Message, Dot9MessageType}` (the H90 protocol)
  and a generic `trpc::msg::{MessageT, Message, MessageType}`. Its methods are
  `sendMIDIRequest()` and `midiReceived()` → **request/response RPC carried over
  MIDI**, defined in `AppCommon/DeviceComms/TRPCMessageBroker.h`.
- **Request/response correlation by message ID.** The broker tracks a
  `pendingResponseID` and logs `Expected Response ID(0x…)`, `Ignoring inbound
  message ID:0x…`, `Corrupt message/response from H90 with ID:…`. A device error
  surfaces as **`Device returned error response code 0x02`** (the response
  carries a status byte; `0x02` = error).
- **Wire payload = FlatBuffers.** Native `…T` types abound
  (`ExportedProgramT/PresetT/ProgramListT/AlgorithmT/DeviceStateT`,
  `ProgramSummaryT`, `trpc::dot9::ParameterMetadataT`, `ValuePair`) alongside
  `flatbuffers::DefaultAllocator` and the log line
  `"SystemParametersResponse flatbuffer size: "`. **No reflection schema
  (`.bfbs`) is embedded** — the schema is compiled in, so the FlatBuffers tables
  had to be recovered from RTTI + JSON keys (catalogued in the YAML), and the
  `Dot9MessageType` enum *values* are not stringified (they'd need deeper RE or a
  live capture).
- **Transport = JUCE UMP SysEx7** (`juce_audio_basics/.../juce_UMPSysEx7.cpp`).
  `sendMIDIRequest` validates that its outgoing buffer is a status-byte stream
  (`cmp first_byte, 0xF0`), i.e. the RPC frame is a **MIDI SysEx** message that
  the JUCE UMP layer packetises for USB/BLE MIDI.

### SysEx header recovered: `F0 1C 77` (manufacturer 0x1C, model 0x77)

The earlier pass guessed the manufacturer header would need a live capture. It
does **not** — it is hard-coded and was recovered from disassembly of the dot9
broker's **inbound parser** (`.text` 0x14013ac90). The parser gates on three
literal bytes before doing anything else:

```asm
0x14013acdd: cmp byte ptr [rcx],     0xf0   ; SysEx start
0x14013ace6: cmp byte ptr [rcx + 1], 0x1c   ; manufacturer = 0x1C  (Eventide, MMA single-byte ID)
0x14013acf0: cmp byte ptr [rcx + 2], 0x77   ; model / sub-id = 0x77  (H90 "dot9")
```

The header-build/parse helpers (`0x140429a40` build, `0x140429c50` parse) seed
the 8-byte header struct with the literal `mov qword ptr [rcx], 0x771cf0`
(little-endian bytes **`F0 1C 77 00`**), then copy four more 7-bit header fields
from buffer offsets `+4..+7`. The constant `F0 1C 77 00` occurs **10×** in
`.text` — in both the `trpc::dot9` and the generic `trpc::msg` broker
instantiations, on **both** the receive parser and the
`sendMIDIRequest` build path (`0x14013ee5c`). So the H90 TRPC envelope is, in
both directions:

```
F0 1C 77 00  <f4> <f5> <f6> <f7>   <FlatBuffers payload…>   F7
└── prefix ─┘└─ 4-byte header ──┘  └─── body ───┘
```

- **`F0 1C 77`** = SysEx start, Eventide manufacturer `0x1C`, H90 dot9 model
  `0x77`. (This is **not** the legacy Factor-family `1C 70`; the Dot9 platform
  uses its own `0x77`.) Offset 3 is a `0x00` separator in the app's framing.
- The four header bytes `f4..f7` are **7-bit** values; the dispatcher
  reconstructs a **14-bit message id** from a high/low pair
  (`movzx eax,[hdr]; shl al,7; or al,[hdr+1]` → `(hi<<7)|lo`) and correlates it
  with the `pendingResponseID`. A reconstructed value of **`0x02`** is the device
  **error response** (matches the log `Device returned error response code 0x02`).

#### Live verification (confirmed on hardware)

The recovered header was **verified against a real H90** over its own
class-compliant USB-MIDI interface (the port H90 Control uses — *not* a
WIDI-bridged port, where the pedal stays silent on SysEx). Reproduce with:

```
go run ./cmd/usb-probe --device h90 --h90-ping
```

`--h90-ping` sends the safest possible Dot9 frame — the recovered prefix, a
zeroed 8-byte header, and an **empty body** (`F0 1C 77 00 00 00 00 00 F7`).
Opcode/type 0 with no payload cannot form any valid TRPC write, so the pedal
**rejects it with the error response** rather than acting on it. The reply
(deterministic across runs) is:

```
F0 1C 77 00 | 01 37 00 02 | 0C 00 00 00 00 01 00 06 … | F7
└ prefix +0 ┘└─ header ──┘└──── FlatBuffers payload ────┘
```

decoded by the probe as
`DOT9 hdr=00 01 37 00 02 status=0x02 (ERROR response) … fb_root_uoffset=12`.

This confirms, on hardware, every piece of the static analysis:

- the **`F0 1C 77`** prefix (the pedal answers it; it ignores the Universal
  Identity Request and the legacy `1C 70` TJ WANTs);
- the **`0x00`** separator at offset 3 (matches the seeded `0x771cf0` constant);
- the header **status byte `0x02`** = device error (matches the
  `Device returned error response code 0x02` log string);
- a **7-bit-clean** body that is a **FlatBuffers buffer** (first u32 = root table
  uoffset = 12).

The Universal Identity Request (`F0 7E 7F 06 01 F7`) gets **no reply** even on
this port — the H90 only speaks its own Dot9 framing.

The rest of the framing pipeline (unchanged from the earlier pass, now with the
prefix pinned down):

1. **7-bit-safe encoding.** id/type bytes are split high/low (`shr al,7` /
   `and al,0x7f`) so every byte stays in MIDI's 0x00–0x7F range; the buffer is
   assembled with **4-byte alignment** and 32-bit little-endian length prefixes.
2. **Optional zlib compression.** If a per-device capability flag is set *and*
   the payload exceeds **100 bytes** (`cmp size, 0x64`), the buffer is
   **deflate-compressed** (`deflateInit2`, windowBits 15, memLevel 8). Small
   payloads go uncompressed.
3. **Segmentation for very large transfers.** Bulk objects (algorithm imports,
   backups) are chunked via
   `H90Device::sendSegmentedPayload(SegmentedPayloadType type, uint batchID,
   uint segmentID, uint8_t* data, size_t len, msg::MessageType)`
   (`"No active batch operation for segmented payload"` /
   `"Error sending segment <n>"`).

The send path (`sendMIDIRequest`, `0x14013ed10`) additionally asserts the frame
it built begins with a status byte (`cmp cl, 0xf0`) and is longer than the
3-byte prefix (`cmp ebx, 3`) before handing it to JUCE's UMP SysEx7 packetiser
(which appends the closing `F7`).

**Still genuinely needs a live capture:** the per-operation `Dot9MessageType`
*numeric opcode values*. The broker is generic and dispatches by **message id
correlation + a registered Listener callback**, not by a static `switch` on the
operation type, and the `Dot9MessageType` enum names are **not** stringified in
the binary (no `EnumNamesDot9MessageType` table — unlike the enums recovered in
`h90-control.yaml`). So the *envelope and field layout are now known*, but which
opcode means `getSystemParameters` vs `changePresetParameter` still has to be
read off the wire.

### Channels, operations, and the verify_control angle

- **Comms backends:** `Dot9/Comms/TideUSB.cpp` (USB) and `LegacyDot9Comms.cpp`
  (legacy HID identity), plus BLE. The same TRPC/SysEx is transport-agnostic, so
  it should work over the H90's **own BLE-MIDI peripheral** (fw ≥ 1.7.5) — the
  endpoint that matches this project's daemon and the natural place for a future
  on-device readback.
- **A documented read exists:** `getSystemParameters` returns a
  `SystemParametersResponse` flatbuffer; `Dot9Device::changePresetParameter(int,
  ValuePair)` is the live single-parameter *write*. Read ops
  (`loadExportedAlgorithm`, `import*`) and write ops (`changePresetParameter`,
  `loadDeviceBackup`, `restoreDevice`, `backupDevice`, `factoryReset`) are
  catalogued in the YAML and flagged. **The daemon must never issue the writes
  during research.**
- **Module map** (`ControlApp/Source/…`): `App/{AppController,UIController,
  SoftwareController,AlgoManager}`, `Device/{H90Device,H90Device-progdb,
  DeviceManager,AbstractDevice}`, `Dot9/{Dot9Controller,Dot9Device,
  Comms/TideUSB,Comms/LegacyDot9Comms}`, `Model/{Parameter,Preset,Program,
  ProgramDetail,ProgramSummary,DeviceInfo}Model`, `Persistence/{DocController,
  ExportedAlgorithmDoc}`.

**Implication for verify_control.** A real on-device readback for the H90 *does*
exist (TRPC `getSystemParameters` / state reads over BLE-MIDI or USB), unlike the
dead SysEx/mass-storage routes above. The **envelope is now fully known**
(`F0 1C 77 00` + 7-bit header + FlatBuffers body + `F7`); what remains before a
working `getSystemParameters` round-trip is (a) the `Dot9MessageType` opcode
value for that request and (b) the `SystemParametersResponse` FlatBuffers field
offsets — both a short live capture away (issue the app's `getSystemParameters`
while sniffing, then diff against the known envelope). Until then, H90
`verify_control` stays on the **BLE-MIDI echo** model
(`internal/engine/feedback.go`). The `h90` track in `cmd/usb-probe` now has a
scaffolded **TRPC/SysEx7 replay path** (`--h90-trpc`, `--h90-raw`,
`--h90-handshake`, `--h90-dev`); seed its frame builder with the recovered
`F0 1C 77 00` prefix so the capture only has to fill in the opcode + payload.

## Confidence summary

- **High confidence (vendor-sourced):** `program` via Program Change, the
  1–100 / 0–99 PC Offset behaviour, the no-Bank-Select fact, the list of
  mappable functions, the `≥64`/`<64` switch rule, MIDI channel/Omni/Output Mode
  behaviour. All from S1/S2 (+S3/S4 forum confirmation).
- **Convention (NOT vendor defaults), must be configured on the H90:** every CC
  number. Function semantics are sourced; the *numbers* are a convention (C1 for
  the navigation/bypass/system block; invented-contiguous for the continuous
  params). Marked `# convention (no factory default)` in the YAML.

## Caveats

- All CC numbers require manual setup on the H90 (Global Control) or H90 Control;
  out of the box none of the CC controls do anything.
- `tuner` is toggle/display-side only — engaging it shows the tuner on the H90's
  screen and does not transmit tuning data back over MIDI (S3).
- The forum threads (S3/S4) reference nearby firmware revisions (1.3.0, 1.7.5,
  1.9.x) for the same Global Mapping Chart; the chart is stable across these.
  Values were taken against **1.7.1** docs to match the project's pinned URL.
- No documented public SysEx for parameter editing was found; if a future need
  arises, capture it via MIDI-learn rather than guessing a SysEx frame.
